subject: ap-world-history
title: Imperialism & Colonialism
stimulus:
“While the world revolution of Westernization [after 1850] created a political world order radically different
from all past human experience, it also disrupted the non-Western societies constituting the bulk of humanity.
. . . The Westerners with their sense of mission also introduced their education. Along the way
[Western-educated intellectuals from non-Western societies] soon acquired a taste for the dominant ideals of
the West, especially the liberal plea for equality, freedom, and self-determination and the socialists’ cry for
social justice for all exploited and oppressed peoples and classes....
Inevitably, the non-Western intellectuals turned their lessons to their own use. The ideals of freedom and
self-determination justified protests over the humiliation of their countries and cultures. As a result of their
Westernization they became anti-Western nationalists, caught in a love-hate attitude toward the West....
The world revolution of Westernization perpetuated inequality and ruinous cultural subversion while at the
same time improving the material conditions of life.”
Theodore von Laue, historian, The World Revolution of Westernization,
1987

parts.prompt:
Identify ONE non-Western nationalist leader whose actions might be used to illustrate the author's argument
in the passage.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for identifying a specific non-Western nationalist leader whose background and actions illustrate von Laue's argument that Western-educated intellectuals became anti-Western nationalists.

required_elements: Educated in or influenced by Western thought
Used Western ideals to justify anti-Western nationalism
alternatives.correct_example: Mohandas Gandhi, who studied law in Britain and was educated in Western liberal thought, illustrates von Laue's argument by using the Western ideals of equality and self-determination to justify India's independence movement against British imperial rule — embodying the 'love-hate attitude toward the West' the author describes.

required_elements: Connection to Western education or ideals
Anti-Western or nationalist dimension of their actions
alternatives.correct_example: Ho Chi Minh, who lived and studied in France and was influenced by Marxist ideas originating in the West, used the Western-derived rhetoric of self-determination (appealing even to Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points) to build the Vietnamese independence movement against French colonial rule, illustrating how Western ideals were 'turned to their own use.'

required_elements: Named non-Western leader whose Western education underpinned nationalist resistance
alternatives.correct_example: Kwame Nkrumah, educated at universities in the United States and Britain, absorbed socialist and Pan-Africanist ideas and then led Ghana's independence movement, using Western political ideologies to challenge Western colonial control — exactly the 'anti-Western nationalist' pattern von Laue identifies.

wrong_examples: A nationalist leader was important in opposing Western imperialism. (Vague — no specific named leader)
Many leaders fought against imperialism in this period. (Listing rather than identifying one specific person with relevant details)
common_traps: Naming a leader without explaining how their actions illustrate von Laue's specific argument about Western-educated anti-Western nationalism
Choosing a pre-1850 leader (outside the passage's time frame)
Choosing a Western leader rather than a non-Western nationalist

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE way in which the “world revolution of Westernization” identified by von Laue in the passage
disrupted non-Western societies.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for explaining a specific, historically defensible way that Westernization disrupted non-Western societies. Must go beyond the passage — cannot simply restate von Laue's general claims. Requires a causal explanation.

required_elements: Causal explanation connecting Westernization to the disruption
alternatives.correct_example: The introduction of Western cash-crop colonial economies disrupted indigenous subsistence agricultural systems across Africa and Asia. Colonial powers like Britain and France forced farmers to grow export commodities (cotton, rubber, indigo) for European markets rather than food for local consumption, which destroyed traditional agricultural patterns and created economic dependence — contributing to famines and the collapse of self-sufficient village economies.

required_elements: Specific form of disruption with causal explanation
Distinct from simply restating the passage's general claims
alternatives.correct_example: The introduction of Western legal systems and property rights disrupted communal land ownership practices in colonized societies. In regions like sub-Saharan Africa and Southeast Asia, traditional systems of communal land use were replaced by individual title deeds that colonial administrators could then seize or sell, dispossessing indigenous communities and undermining the social structures based on collective land stewardship.

required_elements: Specific mechanism of cultural or social disruption with causal explanation
alternatives.correct_example: Western missionary education disrupted indigenous cultural systems by replacing local languages, religious practices, and community knowledge with European ones. Indigenous children educated in mission schools were trained in European languages and Christian values that devalued their own cultures, creating the 'ruinous cultural subversion' von Laue mentions and producing a generation that was psychologically alienated from both their own heritage and the European societies that educated them.

wrong_examples: Westernization disrupted non-Western societies by introducing new ideas. (Too vague — does not explain a specific mechanism of disruption)
The West took over non-Western countries and changed them. (Restates the general premise without explaining a specific disruption mechanism)
common_traps: Simply restating von Laue's general claims without providing a specific, concrete explanation
Describing what Westernization introduced (schools, laws, etc.) without explaining how this disrupted pre-existing social structures
Confusing explanation with identification — must say 'because' or explain causation, not just name what happened

parts.prompt:
Explain ONE reason why historians in the late twentieth century reinterpreted Western imperialism in the
way that von Laue does in the second and third paragraphs of the passage.

scoring_points.description: Earns 1 point for explaining a specific reason why late 20th-century historians reinterpreted imperialism to emphasize non-Western agency, the contradictions of Westernization, or the complexity of colonial encounters. Must identify a historical development that motivated this reinterpretation.

required_elements: Specific historical development motivating the reinterpretation
Causal explanation connecting that development to the scholarly shift
alternatives.correct_example: The wave of decolonization in Asia and Africa following World War II gave newly independent nations and their scholars a political platform to challenge earlier Eurocentric imperial histories. As non-Western historians gained access to Western academic institutions, they produced subaltern studies and postcolonial scholarship that foregrounded non-Western agency and disruption — creating the intellectual context for von Laue's nuanced interpretation of imperialism as simultaneously disruptive and transformative.

required_elements: Connection explaining why this led to reinterpreting imperialism as von Laue does
alternatives.correct_example: The emergence of postcolonial theory in the 1970s–1980s, associated with scholars like Edward Said (Orientalism, 1978), challenged the earlier 'civilizing mission' narratives that portrayed imperialism as purely beneficial. Late 20th-century historians reinterpreted imperialism to acknowledge the internal contradictions von Laue describes — that Western ideals could simultaneously empower colonized peoples to resist colonialism and perpetuate cultural and economic inequality — because this framework aligned with the broader postcolonial critique of Western knowledge production.

required_elements: Explains why this motivated scholars to see imperialism as von Laue does
alternatives.correct_example: The Cold War context of the late 20th century encouraged Western historians to reinterpret imperialism more critically, as both the United States and the Soviet Union competed for influence in newly decolonized nations. American scholars in particular had reason to distance themselves from older apologetic imperial narratives because the U.S. was positioning itself as an anti-colonial power, creating intellectual space for more nuanced interpretations that acknowledged the destructive as well as the modernizing dimensions of Western expansion.

wrong_examples: Historians reinterpreted imperialism because they had new evidence. (Too vague — does not identify a specific development or explain how it motivated the reinterpretation)
Von Laue wrote this because imperialism was important. (Circular — does not explain the historical reason for the late 20th-century reinterpretation)
common_traps: Describing what von Laue's argument says rather than explaining why historians moved toward this type of interpretation
Providing a vague reference to 'new perspectives' without identifying a specific historical development that motivated those perspectives
Conflating von Laue's specific argument with generic statements about historians reconsidering the past
